coredata.dc:title: Exploring green and efficient zero-dimensional carbon-based inhibitors for carbon steel: From performance to mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: He, Haijie
Shi, Jian
Yu, Shuqi
Yang, Jie
Xu, Ke
He, Chuang
Li, Xueqi
coredata.dc:description: Metal corrosion results in significant economic losses and poses potential safety problems. Graphene oxide (GO) is an innovative and effective corrosion inhibitor but suffers from a laborious and financially burdensome post-modification. Herein, zero-dimensional carbon-based graphene quantum dots without and with N doping (namely, GQDs and NGQDs) were synthesized without any post-modification, and innovatively served as a novel green, efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel. Crucially, their inhibition mechanism and the inhibiting enhancement mechanism induced by N incorporation was comprehensively revealed. The results showed that in stark contrast to GO, GQDs and NGQDs without post-treatment possessed smaller sizes, much better monodispersity, and higher water solubility. And they displayed a remarkable inhibition efficiency (η) for carbon steel, which was comparable to the modified GO. Specifically, GQDs exhibited an η of 83.32% at a concentration of 200 mg/L, while NGQDs achieved an even higher η of 89.25%. As evidenced by the corrosion morphology investigation, adsorption isotherm analysis, and theoretical calculation, the inhibition mechanism of GQDs and NGQDs was attributed to their physical and chemical adsorption on the steel surface, hindering metal dissolution of the anode and hydrogen evolution of the cathode. Innovatively, density functional theory and molecular dynamics simulation elucidated the positive impact of N doping on the inhibition performance of NGQDs. That was N doping endowed NGQDs with better charge-donating ability, leading to stronger adsorption on the steel substrate than GQDs. This study not only contributed to addressing a critical need in the industry by presenting a viable environmentally friendly, low-cost, and efficient corrosion inhibitor, but also provided new insights for the development of corrosion inhibition theory.

head.ce:title: Exploring green and efficient zero-dimensional carbon-based inhibitors for carbon steel: From performance to mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal corrosion results in significant economic losses and poses potential safety problems. Graphene oxide (GO) is an innovative and effective corrosion inhibitor but suffers from a laborious and financially burdensome post-modification. Herein, zero-dimensional carbon-based graphene quantum dots without and with N doping (namely, GQDs and NGQDs) were synthesized without any post-modification, and innovatively served as a novel green, efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel. Crucially, their inhibition mechanism and the inhibiting enhancement mechanism induced by N incorporation was comprehensively revealed. The results showed that in stark contrast to GO, GQDs and NGQDs without post-treatment possessed smaller sizes, much better monodispersity, and higher water solubility. And they displayed a remarkable inhibition efficiency (η) for carbon steel, which was comparable to the modified GO. Specifically, GQDs exhibited an η of 83.32% at a concentration of 200 mg/L, while NGQDs achieved an even higher η of 89.25%. As evidenced by the corrosion morphology investigation, adsorption isotherm analysis, and theoretical calculation, the inhibition mechanism of GQDs and NGQDs was attributed to their physical and chemical adsorption on the steel surface, hindering metal dissolution of the anode and hydrogen evolution of the cathode. Innovatively, density functional theory and molecular dynamics simulation elucidated the positive impact of N doping on the inhibition performance of NGQDs. That was N doping endowed NGQDs with better charge-donating ability, leading to stronger adsorption on the steel substrate than GQDs. This study not only contributed to addressing a critical need in the industry by presenting a viable environmentally friendly, low-cost, and efficient corrosion inhibitor, but also provided new insights for the development of corrosion inhibition theory.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal materials, especially carbon steels, are extensively employed in construction and industrial fields, benefiting from their exceptional mechanical properties and remarkable economy [1]. Unfortunately, metal corrosion, known as the “cancer of metals”, inevitably results in tremendous economic losses, and even serious security accidents [2]. Approximately 100 years ago, the loss of metal corrosion reached up to 580 million pounds [3]. Since then, it has become increasingly crucial to implement corrosion protection technologies to mitigate metal corrosion. Up to now, numerous methods, including cathodic protection [4], anti-corrosion coatings [5], and inhibitors [6] have been developed. Among them, corrosion inhibitors are recognized as the most effective and simplest solution owing to their flexible operation and low cost [6,7]. Moreover, corrosion inhibitors are widely applied in various aggressive acidic surroundings, including surface pickling, and oil well acidification industries. However, traditional commercial corrosion inhibitors such as chromates, phosphates, and nitrates are toxic, and fail to meet environmental protection requirements [8]. To overcome these shortcomings, novel corrosion inhibitors such as amino acids, ionic liquids (ILs), and plant extracts are applied [9–14]. While these organic inhibitors are more prevalent and environmentally acceptable, they normally involve tedious preparation processes for plant extracts and the high cost of raw materials for ionic liquids and amino acids, restricting their further application [15,16]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop cost-effective and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors to protect metal materials against corrosion.

Graphene oxide (GO), a unique two-dimensional material, possesses several advantages such as cost-effectiveness [17,18], environmental friendliness [19], high surface area [20], and exceptional corrosion resistance [5], expected to act as an ideal corrosion inhibitor. For instance, Saleh’s group synthesized a GO-based inhibitor from waste graphite, achieving a maximum inhibition efficiency of over 80% for carbon steel in a 15% HCl corrosive medium, thus effectively addressing both corrosion-related challenges and waste disposal issues [21]. Up to now, GO has been extensively reported in numerous studies as an effective corrosion inhibitor for Fe and Cu metals in both acidic and neutral environments [22–27]. Although GO exhibit practical corrosion inhibition properties, its unfavorable water solubility challenges further application, inevitably requiring a tedious post-modification [28]. For example, Quraishi and colleagues prepared surfactant-modified GO inhibitors by heating sodium dodecyl sulfate, sodium propyl sulfate and GO at 90 °C for 72 h [22]. Subsequently, Mobin et al. prepared an IL-functionalized GO inhibitor via stirring ILs and GO at 60 °C for 48 h [29]. Besides, Babaei, Chen, and other researchers also employed single-step or multi-step methods to functionalize GO toward improving its water solubility [22–27]. However, the aforementioned post-modification is time-consuming and cost-increasing, undermining the further application of GO-based corrosion inhibitors.

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as zero-dimensional flat fragments of GO [30], have sparked tremendous research interest by virtue of their favorable features including low toxicity, chemical inertia, and stable photoluminescence. Although GQDs have been extensively considered in optical and electronic devices, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and sensing [31–34], they fail to be applied in the field of corrosion inhibition. Moreover, compared to GO, GQDs possessed more abundant surface groups, excellent solubility and dispersibility, and better tunability in chemical-physical properties [34]. Therefore, there are considerable reasons to believe that GQDs can function as novel, green, and low-cost corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel. Furthermore, it is well known that the N introduction is able to remarkably promote the inhibition capacity of graphene-related materials. For example, Ren et al. incorporated the N element into graphene and successfully mitigated metal corrosion [35]. Similarly, Saleh’s group functionalized GO with N-riched diamino-decane and diamino-dodecane, achieving a maximum inhibition efficiency of 84% for carbon steel [21]. These researches convincingly evidence that incorporating the N element into GQDs (namely, preparing NGQDs) will conduce to enhancing their inhibition capacity for metal corrosion. In addition, the in-depth study of the inhibition mechanism has become increasingly significant for corrosion inhibitors [36]. Consequently, it is desirable to investigate the inhibition capacity of GQDs and NGQDs as corrosion inhibitors and the related inhibition mechanism.

Herein, zero-dimensional GQDs and NGQDs were synthesized without any post-modification and innovatively served as a novel green, efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel. more importantly, their inhibition mechanism as well as the N doping-caused inhibiting improvement mechanism was deeply uncovered. Specifically, GQDs and NGQDs owned smaller sizes and higher dispersion stability than GO. And the effectiveness of GQDs and NGQDs as corrosion inhibitors was demonstrated through weight loss, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests. Both GQDs and NGQDs presented favorable inhibition performance, comparable to modified GO. While NGQDs performed a better inhibition efficiency (η) of 89.25% than GQDs (83.32%). Their inhibition mechanism was elucidated through corrosion morphology investigation, adsorption isotherm analysis, and theoretical calculation. This work not only presented a viable environmentally friendly, low-cost, and efficient corrosion inhibitor but also provided new insights for the future development of corrosion inhibition theory.

Naphthalene, concentrated nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia, and concentrated hydrochloride acid (HCl) were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Q235 carbon steel is consisted of the following weight percentages: 0.21 C, 0.035 Si, 0.25 Mn, 0.082 P, with Fe constituting the remaining composition. Prior to experiments, all surfaces of Q235 carbon steel specimens were sequentially polished using sandpaper ranging from #600 to #2000, and washed by deionized (DI) water and alcohol.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal approach, with slight adjustments derived from the previous work [37]. First, nitrated naphthalene was produced by adding 3.0 g naphthalene to 80 mL concentrated HNO3 and stirring magnetically for 10 h at 80 °C. Subsequent to centrifugation and desiccation, nitro naphthalene was acquired.

For the synthesis of GQDs, a total of 1.2 g powdered nitro naphthalene was added in 100 mL 0.1 M NaOH. The resulting mixture was then heated at 200 °C for 8 h in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Following a heating process, the obtained product underwent a series of purification steps including neutralization, centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis to eliminate unnecessary byproducts. Subsequently, rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying techniques were adopted to further reduce the impurities. Ultimately, dark-brown solid GQDs were obtained.

In the case of NGQDs synthesis, the saturated ammonia solution with a volume of 27 mL as the nitrogen source was used to replace an equal volume of 0.1 M NaOH. The remaining processing steps were unchanged.

In our study, GQDs/NGQDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method, reflecting a streamlined procedure. On the contrary, the synthesis of GO in previous literature was a time-consuming and complicated process. For example, in one approach, the initial step involved the preparation of graphene nanosheets, achieved by introducing potassium peroxodisulphate, phosphorus pentoxide, and natural graphite into sulfuric acid, followed by four days of reflux at 80 °C. Subsequently, the modification of GO through acylation and surfactant attachment introduced additional layers of complexity [22]. Similarly, in another literature discussing a modified-GO inhibitor [29], the process was intricate, involving a two-step procedure with a meticulous combination of L-proline and nitric acid, extended heating, vacuum drying, and IL formation for enhanced water solubility. Therefore, this paper provided a more convenient preparation method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were utilized to determine the morphologies of both GQDs and NGQDs. For the characterization of their structures and components, Raman spectra, Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR), and X-ray Photoelectron spectra (XPS) were adopted. In detail, TEM was conducted through a Tecnai F20 field-emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (FEI, USA). For TEM testing, the sample was prepared as follows: GQDs were diluted in DI water, then dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid, and finally dried in the air. FTIR spectra were recorded utilizing a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, USA), spanning the range of 500–4000 cm−1 through KBr compressed discs. XPS was performed using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA) with an Al Kα 280 eV radiation source, and the C1s peak was calibrated to a binding energy of 284.8 eV.

To clarify the long-term dispersion stability of GQDs and NGQDs in 1 M HCl solution, Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) were conducted. Specifically, UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired by adopting a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Lambda 750, USA) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. PL spectra were measured with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) utilizing a 1 cm quartz cell, and conducted at room temperature.

The weight loss test was carried out according to the ASTM standard G 31–72 [38]. Q235 carbon steel specimens with dimensions of 15 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm were employed. Prior to immersion in various test solutions, all specimens were weighed three times. The test solutions with concentrations of 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg/L GQDs/NGQDs were prepared by directly introducing GQDs and NGQDs into 1 M HCl. The specimens were removed from the solutions every 24 h, thoroughly cleaned, dried, and weighed. Subsequently, the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency (η) were calculated using the following equations [39,40].

∆W, A, and t in the provided equations represented the average weight loss, the surface area, and the immersion time of the specimen, respectively. Q235 carbon steel corrosion rates in 1 M HCl solution with and without GQDs/NGQDs were denoted by W corr and Wꞌcorr, respectively.

PARSTAT 4000 + electrochemical workstation was used to measure electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP). A standard three-electrode system was employed, including Q235 carbon steel as the working electrode (WE) with an exposure area of 1 cm2, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode (RE), and a platinum plate as the counter electrode (CE). The preparation method of test solutions for electrochemical measurement was the same as that for weight loss tests. A steady state of open circuit potential (OCP) was expected prior to starting the tests. EIS measurements were conducted at 10 mV amplitude between 105 and 10−2 Hz, and PDP was executed at 1 mV s−1 by scanning the potential range within ± 200 mV versus OCP. The ZSimDemo 3.30 was used to fit and analyze the EIS data. To ensure reproducibility, every electrochemical experiment was carried out three times.

To observe corrosion morphology, Q235 carbon steel specimens were washed, dried, and placed in a vacuum oven after the weight loss measurement. The corrosion morphology of the steel specimen was examined using the Keyence VR-6000 3D scanning instrument, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS). In detail, a FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 (USA) was employed, conducting SEM topography analysis with an acceleration voltage of 3 kV, and energy spectrum mapping analysis at 15 kV. Additionally, the surface states of the steel specimen were determined using the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS (USA).

Material Studio 7.0 software was used for molecular simulation calculation. The molecule models of GQDs and NGQDs were constructed based on their characterized structures by FTIR and XPS. The prepared GQDs and NGQDs molecules were optimized using the Dmol3 module. The B3LYP method was adopted at the DFT level, and DND3.5 was used as the base set. The convergence quality was set to fine with an energy tolerance value of 2.0e−5 Ha and a maximum displacement of 0.005 Å. The SCF tolerance was 1.0e−5. To examine the correlation between molecular properties and corrosion inhibition behaviors, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the energy gap (ΔE = E HOMO
E LUMO) were analyzed.

Furthermore, the Forcite module was used to carry out the molecular dynamic (MD) simulation, and the adsorption energy (E binding) between GQDs/NGQDs and Fe(110) on the carbon steel surface was obtained. The simulation box containing 1 inhibitor molecule, 300 H2O molecules, and 6 layers of Fe (110) was established to determine the interaction between the studied molecule and Fe substrate. The Fe (110) interface model with a dimension of 19.86 Å × 19.86 Å × 40.13 Å contained 435 Fe atoms overall. Periodic boundary conditions, COMPASSII force field, and NVT system were used for MD simulation. The position of the iron atom was fixed in the simulation. The time step was 1 ps, and the total simulation time was 1000 ps.

In the present study, GQDs and NGQDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal approach without any post-modification ( Fig. 1.). TEM was utilized to characterize particle size distributions and lattice structures of GQDs and NGQDs. As revealed by TEM images ( Fig. 2 a and b), both GQDs and NGQDs exhibited quasi-spherical shapes and were well dispersed without any agglomeration. They demonstrated similar particle sizes with average diameters of 2.63 nm and 2.96 nm, respectively. Moreover, HRTEM images further confirmed the presence of crystalline structures in both GQDs and NGQDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [41]. The Raman spectra of GQDs and NGQDs ( Fig. 3 a) exhibited two distinct peaks at around 1344 and 1582 cm−1, representing the D band associated with sp3 hybrid carbon and the G band corresponding to sp2 carbon, respectively. I D/I G values of GQDs (0.87) and NGQDs (0.82) were lower than 1, further verifying their high crystallinity, which was coincident with the observation of HRTEM.

The comprehensive information concerning surface chemical states and elemental compositions of GQDs and NGQDs were revealed by FTIR and XPS. As depicted in FTIR spectra ( Fig. 3 b), GQDs contained obvious peaks at 3444,1388, and 1592 cm−1, corresponding to OH, COH, and CC/CO respectively; While the peaks at 1265 and 1070 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration bands of C−O−C [42]. This indicated the presence of diverse O-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs. For NGQDs, besides O-containing groups (−OH, CO, and C–O−C at 3243, 1762, and 1034 cm−1 respectively), new peaks at 3056 and 1289 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of N − H, and the weak peak at 1421 cm−1 belonged to C−N [37]. These N-containing groups manifested the successful introduction of the N element into NGQDs. In addition, the stretching vibration of CC bonds confirmed the sp2 C structure in GQDs and NGQDs, in accordance with TEM and Raman analyses. These findings of FTIR spectra were further testified by XPS characterization. In the full-range XPS spectra of GQDs and NGQDs ( Fig. 4 a), distinct peaks corresponding to O1s (532 eV), N1s (400 eV), and C1s (284 eV) were observed, along with an additional peak from impurity Na + (496 eV). It must be noted that the N 1 s peak in GQDs was ascribed to residual nitric acid from the synthesis process. Thus, there was no need to fit it. Following N doping, the contents of O and C elements in NGQDs decreased, while the N content noticeably increased ( Table 1). This was probably attributed to the substitution of N-containing groups for O-containing groups. In the high-resolution C1s spectra of GQDs and NGQDs (Figs. 4 b and 4 e), three distinct peaks were assigned to CC/CC (284.4 ± 0.5 eV), CO (285.9 ± 0.5 eV), and CO (288.3 ± 0.5 eV) bonds [43]. CC/C–C bonds accounting for roughly 70% in both GQDs and NGQDs indicated abundant sp2 C in their structures, in accordance with the results of TEM and Raman. The O1s spectra (Figs. 4 c and 4 f) involved two fitting peaks corresponding to CO (530.7 ± 0.5 eV) and CO (532.5 ± 0.2 eV) bonds [43]. Significantly, the content of CO peak in NGQDs showed a notable reduction than that of GQDs, suggesting the replacement of carboxyl groups by amine groups after N incorporation. The N1s spectra (Fig. 4 d) of NGQDs revealed the presence of NH2 (399.1 eV), pyrrole-N (400.3 eV), and graphite-N (401.8 eV) [37,44], implying the existence of N in the surface and carbon core of NGQDs. In brief, the conclusions of FTIR and XPS spectra uncovered that abundant functional groups endowed GQDs and NGQDs with better water solubility [45] than GO, providing a necessary precondition for their application as highly effective corrosion inhibitors. Additionally, N doping was expected to further improve the corrosion inhibition performance of NGQDs.

The optical properties of GQDs and NGQDs were identified by UV–vis and PL spectra. In Fig. 5 a and c, the UV–vis spectra exhibited a prominent peak at 297 ± 1 nm and a weak peak at 352 ± 1 nm, ascribed to ππ * transitions of sp2 domains and nπ * transitions of O-/N-based groups, respectively [43]. After exposure to a 365 nm UV light, the GQDs aqueous solution emitted a light green fluorescent, whereas the NGQDs aqueous solution presented a dark green color. The PL spectra, (Figs. 5 b and 5 d), illustrated that GQDs and NGQDs shared an excitation-independent PL behavior, with the excitation/emission peaks at 470/501 nm for GQDs and 470/507 nm for NGQDs. These optical characteristics offered a novel method to evaluate the dispersion stability of GQDs and NGQDs in solutions.

To sum up, the analyses above demonstrated the successful synthesis of GQDs and NGQDs with average diameters of less than 3 nm, high crystalline carbon cores, and multitudes of functional groups. In sharp contrast to GO (a size of more than 200 nm, and easily stacked together) [22], GQDs and NGQDs without any post-modification possessed smaller sizes, better monodisperse, and higher water solubility (induced by abundant O-/N-containing groups). Thus, GQDs/NGQDs were expected to present a better corrosion inhibition effect than GO. In addition, GQDs and NGQDs shared comparable diameters, and similar crystalline structures, beneficial to further investigate the influence of N incorporation on inhibiting performance of GQDs.

As is well known, the application of corrosion inhibitors requires desirable dispersion stability [46]. Under the premise of the optical properties of GQDs and NGQDs, the optical photographs, UV–vis and PL spectra of various 1 M HCl solutions with various concentrations of GQDs and NGQDs were employed to reflect the dispersion stability of GQDs and NGQDs. As shown in Fig. 6, the colors of solutions laid for 2 weeks at room temperature changed from colorless (0 mg/L) to faint yellow (200 mg/L) with the increase of GQDs/NGQDs in the daylight. Regardless of the concentration of GQDs/NGQDs, there were no sediments in the solutions, substantiating the favorable long-term dispersion stability of GQDs/NGQDs in the HCl solution. It should be mentioned that the agglomeration of GQDs/NGQDs tends to weaken, and even quench their PL on account of the aggregation-caused quenching effect [47]. When exposed to UV light, the solution without GQDs/NGQDs failed to emit PL, while the green PL gradually enhanced with the augment of GQDs/NGQDs, further evidencing the great long-term dispersion stability of GQDs/NGQDs in 1 M HCl. Apart from the qualitative analysis above, UV–vis and maximum PL spectra before and after 2-week standing were measured to quantificationally corroborate the long-term dispersion stability of GQDs/NGQDs in 1 M HCl. As displayed in Fig. 7 a and c, the intensities of UV–vis absorption peaks (at around 300 nm for GQDs, 320 nm for NGQDs) enhanced with increasing GQDs/NGQDs content. Even after 2 weeks of standing, the peak intensities remained unchanged. The maximum PL emission spectra of GQDs and NGQDs (Figs. 7 b and 7 d) shared the same observation with the UV–vis spectra. These results from quantitative analysis further evidenced the long-term dispersion stability of GQDs/NGQDs in 1 M HCl, coinciding with qualitative observations. Worth mentioning, the UV–vis absorption peaks and maximum PL emission peaks in 1 M HCl exhibited noticeable shifts when compared to their behavior in an aqueous solution, possibly ascribed to H+-induced protonation on GQDs/NGQDs [48]. The qualitative and quantitative analyses substantiated that GQDs and NGQDs without the time-consuming and cost-increasing post-modification were equipped with excellent long-term dispersion stability in 1 M HCl solutions. This could lay a solid foundation for the potential application of GQDs and NGQDs as novel, green, and low-cost corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in HCl solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition performance of GQDs and NGQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss method was a highly credible route to assess corrosion inhibition behavior, benefiting from its remarkable precision, ease of use, and consistent results [49,50]. Fig. 8 and Table 2 demonstrated the inhibition efficiency (η) of GQDs and NGQDs in 1 M HCl at different contents after various immersion times. With the heightening GQDs/NGQDs concentration, the η value showed a significant uptrend, denoting steel corrosion was inhibited. When the concentration was 25 mg/L, the η value was 29.19% for GQDs and 62.30% for NGQDs. As the concentration increased to 200 mg/L, the η value improved significantly, reaching 64.65% for GQDs and 83.91% for NGQDs. On the other hand, with extended immersion time, GQDs/NGQDs at 200 mg/L achieved a higher η value of 83.32%/89.25% after 96-h immersion, signifying that GQDs/NGQDs offered long-term protection for carbon steel in 1 M HCl. Crucially, the η value of the reported GO inhibitor was around 70% at 100 mg/L [23,24,29], less than GQDs (76.20%) and NGQDs (86.43%) at the same concentration, highlighting that GQDs and NGQDs were more suitable to be novel, green and low-cost corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in HCl solution than GO. Additionally, N-doped NGQDs had a higher η value than GQDs under the same concentration and immersion time. Especially, the concentration of 25 mg/L, the η value of NGQDs was almost twice that of GQDs, manifesting that N incorporation could significantly improve the corrosion inhibition behavior of GQDs.

EIS was executed to investigate the characteristics of the interface between carbon steel and solutions with and without GQDs/NGQDs. According to Nyquist plots in Fig. 9 a and d (enlarged plots in Fig. S1), the diameters of the capacitive arcs enlarged with the addition of GQDs/NGQDs. And as the GQDs/NGQDs concentration rose, the related diameter gradually increased, suggesting that the charge transfer process at steel surface was more effectively inhibited [36,51]. Besides, the shapes of capacitive arcs had no notable alteration whether GQDs/NGQDs were added or not, suggesting that GQDs and NGQDs did not change the electrochemical characteristics of the Q235 carbon steel electrode [52]. As is well known, greater impedance modulus values suggest enhanced corrosion resistance [53]. As illustrated in Figs. 9 b and 9 e, the impedance modulus over the entire frequency range increased with the addition of GQDs/NGQDs, indicating that the corrosion was markedly inhibited under the protection of GQDs/NGQDs. Correspondingly, compared with the reference sample without GQDs/NGQDs (35.7 Ω∙cm2), the impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz (|Z|0.01 Hz) for 200 mg/L GQDs group (272.6 Ω∙cm2) and 200 mg/L NGQDs group (298.3 Ω∙cm2) represented a remarkable enhancement of 87% and 88%, respectively. Furthermore, the Bode-phase peaks advanced with the increasing content of GQDs/NGQDs, revealing a stronger capacitive response at the carbon steel/solution interface, which was attributed to the adsorption of GQDs/NGQDs [54]. To gain a better understanding of the corrosion process, the EIS data was fitted by the equivalent circuit model in Fig. S2. The resulting parameters, including solution resistance (R s), polarization resistance (R p), and double-layer capacitance (CPEdl) were documented in Table 3. The R s values remained stable after adding GQDs/NGQDs, signifying little influence of GQDs/NGQDs on R s. The R p values were applied to calculate the inhibition efficiency (γ): γ = (R p - R p 0)/R p × 100%, where R p and R p 0 denoted the polarization resistances with and without GQDs/NGQDs, respectively. The addition of GQDs/NGQDs led to an increase in γ values. Within the concentration of 200 mg/L, the higher the GQDs/NGQDs concentration, the higher the γ value, indicating that the inhibiting effect became stronger with the increase of GQDs/NGQDs concentration. The γ values achieved maximum values of 82.29% and 88.00% at 200 mg/L GQDs and 200 mg/L NGQDs solutions within the existing concentration range, consistent with the results from the weight loss tests. Moreover, NGQDs possessed higher γ values than GQDs regardless of the content, demonstrating that NGQDs exhibited better inhibition performance than GQDs. Generally, the CPEdl values are correlated with the corrosion area, and the error parameter n (−1< n <1) signifies the surface homogeneity. The higher concentrations of GQDs/NGQDs resulted in the lower CPEdl values and the larger n values. This inferred that a higher concentration of GQDs/NGQDs made them more adsorption and larger coverage area on the steel surface, thus forming a homogeneous carbon steel surface under this efficient protection [53].

PDP plots (Figs. 9 c and 9 f) were employed to further evaluate the anodic and cathodic processes and assess the inhibiting performance of GQDs and NGQDs. With the introduction of GQDs/NGQDs, the cathodic and anodic branches of the polarization curves shifted to lower corrosion current density (i corr) values, suggesting that the GQDs and NGQDs inhibited both cathodic and anodic reactions [55]. The calculated parameters consisting of the corrosion potential (E corr), i corr, cathode slope (β c), anode slope (β a), and inhibition efficiency (IE) were listed in Table 4. As the GQDs/NGQDs concentration augmented, the E corr moved to the anodic direction within 85 mV (from −481 to −463 mV), suggesting that GQDs and NGQDs were mixed-type inhibitors [8,56]. Significantly, both β c and β a values were reduced by the addition of GQDs/NGQDs. And β a values decreased faster than β c values, implying that GQDs and NGQDs functioned as mixed-type inhibitors, while inhibited the anodic dissolution reaction more effectively. The i corr values were applied to calculated IE values by the following formula: IE= (i corr(0) - i corr(inh))/i corr(0)× 100%, where i corr(0) and i corr(inh) represented the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of GQDs/NGQDs, respectively. The i corr values sharply decreased in the presence of GQDs/NGQDs. Simultaneously, the IE values were proportional to the GQDs/NGQDs concentration, reaching 76.53% and 87.05% for GQDs and NGQDs at 200 mg/L concentration. These observations demonstrated that GQDs and NGQDs hindered the reaction at both cathodic and anodic sites, thus effectively inhibiting carbon steel corrosion. Moreover, NGQDs exhibited higher IE values than GQDs at each concentration, representing an enhanced corrosion inhibition performance after N doping, which was in accordance with the EIS results.

In summary, the corrosion inhibition performance determined through weight loss, EIS, and PDP measurements substantiated that GQDs and NGQDs effectively inhibited the carbon steel corrosion in 1 M HCl by hindering both cathodic and anodic reactions. Significantly, the inhibition capacities of GQDs and NGQDs at 100 mg/L were 73.72% and 84.55% respectively, comparable to the modified GO under similar conditions (shown in Table S1 for more details). This demonstrated that GQDs and NGQDs synthesized without the time-consuming and cost-increasing process could serve as efficient corrosion inhibitors, and possessed a promising application. Additionally, NGQDs held higher inhibition behaviors than GQDs regardless of concentrations, revealing that the N incorporation was an efficient way to enhance the corrosion inhibition property of GQDs.

The inhibition mechanism of GQDs and NGQDs-based inhibitors was uncovered by the corrosion morphology investigation, adsorption isotherm analysis, and theoretical calculation. Innovatively, the inhibition mechanism at the molecular scale and the positive impact of N doping on the inhibition performance were elucidated by quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations.

To investigate the inhibition mechanism of the GQDs/NGQDs-based corrosion inhibitors, as well as the influence mechanism of N incorporation, the surfaces of diverse specimens undergoing 96-h immersion in 1 M HCl solution were characterized by SEM, EDS, 3D scanning instrument, and XPS. 2D corrosion morphology was revealed by SEM and EDS analyses. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the original carbon steel specimen possessed a smooth surface with only a few polishing-induced scratches. After immersion in 1 M HCl corrosive solution, all specimens were subjected to varying degrees of corrosion (Fig. 10 b-d). Without protection from GQDs/NGQDs-based corrosion inhibitors, the steel substrate was severely corroded, forming irregular and loose corrosion products with dense cracks (Fig. 10 b). With the addition of 200 mg/L GQDs/NGQDs, the specimens displayed smoother corrosion surfaces, accompanied by uniformly distributed corrosion products (Fig. 10 c and d), manifesting excellent corrosion protection induced by GQDs and NGQDs. According to the EDS results in Table S2, the predominant elements of the original carbon steel were Fe and C, with a slight amount of O mainly originating from oxidation. After exposure to HCl solution without GQDs/NGQDs, Fe component exhibited a significant decline from 94.94% to 70.71%, while the O component experienced a substantial increase from 0.72% to 22.93%. These alterations were attributed to the formation of numerous corrosion products, including α-FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, and Fe3O4 [57]. The addition of GQDs/NGQDs resulted in a visible increase in Fe proportion (from 70.10% to 89.11%/89.13% GQDs/NGQDs) and a remarkable decrease in O proportion (from 22.93% to 1.95%/2.78% for GQDs/NGQDs) compared to those of the specimen without GQDs/NGQDs protection. This demonstrated the reduction of corrosion products on the steel surface with the incorporation of GQDs/NGQDs, in accordance with the SEM results. The 3D scanning instrument was adopted to clarify 3D corrosion morphology and provide detailed information from the perspective of surface roughness. As illustrated in Fig. 10 e, the original steel sample had a surface roughness parameter (S a) of 0.746 µm. Evidently, after 96-h immersion in 1 M HCl (Fig. 10 f), the steel specimen was severely corroded with the formation of multiple corrosion pits; the depth of the largest pit reached approximately 0.04 mm, leading to a greater S a value of 14.166 µm (Fig. 10 j). GQDs and NGQDs endowed the sample surface with smaller S a values of 1.500 and 1.108 µm (Figs. 10 g and 10 h), which reduced by 89.4% and 92.2% respectively compared to the sample without GQDs/NGQDs protection. The 3D morphology analyses further corrated the 2D analyses, confirming that the GQDs and NGQDs effectively inhibited the corrosion of carbon steel in 1 M HCl, while NGQDs were equipped with better inhibition capacity.

The XPS analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition of the steel surface after 96-h exposure to a 1 M HCl solution in the presence and absence of 200 mg/L GQDs/NGQDs. As shown in Table S3, the survey XPS spectra of the steel samples indicated the primary presence of C, Fe, and O elements on the steel surfaces. Significantly, GQDs and NGQDs samples exhibited higher C element proportions 52.50% and 62.09%, as well as lower Fe element contents of 11.50% and 7.64% separately, compared with blank sample (37.24% C and 16.50% Fe elements). This possibly signified that GQDs and NGQDs successfully adsorbed onto the steel surface, and covered a wide range of surfaces, thus beneficial to resisting corrosion of HCl. Moreover, with the addition of GQDs and NGQDs, the O element proportion decreased by more than 10% in comparison with the blank group (from 46.26% to 36.00% and 30.27%, respectively), testifying that the formation of corrosion products was hindered by GQDs and NGQDs. The evolution of the high-resolution Fe and O spectra from various samples were displayed in Fig. 11. As shown in Fig. 11 a-c, the Fe 2p spectra could be fitted with two peaks at 710.0 ± 0.1 eV and 712.1 ± 0.1 eV, assigned to Fe2O3 and FeOOH [51,58], respectively. In Fig. 11 d-f, the O 1 s spectra were split into three peaks at 529.8 ± 0.1 eV, 531.9 ± 0.1 eV, and 533.4 ± 0.1 eV, associated with O2-, OH- and H2O [58,59]. By calculating the split peak areas, the peak area ratios of Fe(ox)/Fe(hyd) and OH-/O2- were listed in Table S3. The Fe(ox)/Fe(hyd) ratio denoted the ratio of Fe2O3/FeOOH. In the presence of GQDs and NGQDs, the proportion of FeOOH significantly decreased. And the corresponding Fe(ox)/Fe(hyd) ratios of GQDs and NGQDs (2.70 and 3.03 respectively) were much larger than that of the blank sample (0.37), demonstrating that a more compact and protective layer of iron oxides was formed under the protection of GQDs/NGQDs [51]. For another thing, OH-/O2- values of the GQDs and the NGQDs samples were 1.52 and 1.39 respectively, lower than the blank sample (1.79). This further evidenced the generation of more stable iron oxide layers on the steel surface in the presence of GQDs/NGQDs [59,60]. In brief, these findings from XPS implied that GQDs and NGQDs-based inhibitors could adsorb on the steel surface, leading to the formation of a more protective iron oxide layer to resist HCl corrosion. Besides, NGQDs provided a better protective layer than GQDs.

To sum up, the comprehensive corrosion morphology analyses evidenced that the corrosion products were distinctly reduced, and the steel surface became smoother and more homogeneous after the introduction of GQDs/NGQDs. This was attributed to the adsorption of GQDs/NGQDs onto the steel surface, creating a more protective layer of iron oxide. Furthermore, N incorporation endowed NGQDs with more effective adsorption onto the carbon steel surface than GQDs, thereby forming a better protective layer.

To better comprehend the interaction between inhibitors and the metal surface, appropriate adsorption isotherms were identified. According to the Equation: C inh/θ = 1/Kads+C inh (θ was the surface coverage from EIS and PDP results shown in Table 3 and Table 4; C inh and K ads represented the inhibitor concentration and the adsorption equilibrium constant respectively) [61], both the EIS and PDP results yielded the best fit of Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, demonstrating an ideal linear correlation with R2 (of 0.9828 and 0.9268 for GQDs, 0.9922 and 0.9892 for NGQDs), as shown in Fig. 12. Generally speaking, adsorption types consist of physical adsorption, chemical adsorption and both. The adsorption-free energy (ΔG 0 ads) was applied to identify the adroption type of corrosion inhibitors, and was calculated by the Equation: ΔG 0 ads = −RTln(1000K ads) (R was the molar gas constant of 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, and T denoted the absolute temperature of 298 K). Generally, a ΔG 0 ads value of more than − 20 kJ/mol denotes physical adsorption; while a ΔG 0 ads value of less than − 40 kJ/mol indicates chemical adsorption [39]. The calculated ΔG 0 ads values from EIS and PDP measurements were − 25.09 and − 24.40 kJ/mol for GQDs, and − 26.20 and − 26.02 kJ/mol for GQDs respectively. These findings suggested that GQDs/NGQDs could adsorb on metal surfaces through a combination of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption.

The inhibition mechanism of GQDs/NGQDs-based corrosion inhibitors at the molecular scale was further elucidated through theoretical calculations, including quantum chemical calculations and MD simulations. Regarding quantum chemical calculations, they could reveal the relationship between the molecule structure and the corrosion inhibition performance. Considering the deployment of GQDs/NGQDs-based inhibitors in the liquid phase, the optimal adsorption configuration of GQDs and NGQDs was simulated in the liquid environment. According to their characterization, GQDs were composed of high-crystallized carbon core, and numerous O-containing groups (mainly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups); While NGQDs possessed a similar carbon core structure, a limited number of carboxyl groups, and multitudes of hydroxyl, amino groups, pyrrole-N, and graphite-N. Taking these into account, simplified and optimized structure of GQDs and NGQDs were established, and the charge density distribution of HOMO (highest occupied molecule orbit) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecule orbit) were shown in Fig. 13. The HOMO and LUMO spread on the entire GQDs/NGQDs molecules, contributing to their parallel adsorption on the steel surface, thereby achieving the maximum protection area for steel surface in corrosive media [53,62]. According to frontier molecular theory, HOMO and LUMO are always employed to assess the capacity of corrosion inhibitor molecules to receive and lose electrons. Specifically, a low E LUMO value indicates a strong electron-accepting ability, whereas a high E HOMO value molecule signifies better electron-donating capacity [63]. Hence, the difference value between E HOMO and E LUMO (namely, the energy gap (ΔE)) illustrates the charge donation ability of inhibitor molecules to metal surfaces. In other words, the smaller the value of ΔE, the greater the inhibition effect of inhibitors [62,63]. As listed in Table 5, NGQDs owned a lower ΔE value (−3.133 eV) than GQDs (−3.532 eV). This indicated that N-doping conferred a better charge-donating ability on NGQDs, thus improving the potential inhibiting ability of NGQDs.

In terms of MD simulations, they were employed to disclose the interaction between corrosion inhibitor molecules and metal substrates. Fig. 14 demonstrated the side and top views of equilibrium adsorption configurations of GQDs and NGQDs on Fe (110) substrate. GQDs and NGQDs were parallelly adsorbed, contributing to accomplishing the maximum coverage area on the carbon steel surface, thereby exerting an effective inhibiting effect in a corrosive solution. This observation coincided with the results from quantum chemical calculations. This parallel adsorption of GQDs/NGQDs facilitated the electron transfer between their lone pair electrons and the unoccupied 3d orbital of the Fe atom to form coordination bonds [62]. It is well known that the E binding value serves as an indicator of the interaction between inhibitor molecules and the metal surface, and a more negative value E binding value indicates a stronger interaction [51]. As listed in Table 5, the E binding value of NGQDs was − 280.53 kJ∙mol−1, higher than that of GQDs (−265.05 kJ∙mol−1). This manifested that N incorporation enhanced the adsorption ability on the steel substrate, thus improving the corrosion inhibition ability of NGQDs. Combining the quantum chemical calculations and MD simulation, both GQDs and NGQDs could form coordination bonds to strongly adsorb onto the carbon steel surface, thus exhibiting excellent corrosion inhibition performance for carbon steel. Besides, N doping endowed NGQDs molecules with better charge-donating ability and stronger adsorption on the steel surface. This more efficiently improved the corrosion inhibition of NGQDs, coinciding with the experimental results.

Based on the observation above, a rational model ( Fig. 15 ) was proposed to provide a comprehensive understanding of the underlying inhibition mechanisms of GQDs and NGQDs. After immersing it in a blank 1 M HCl solution (Fig. 15 a), Q235 carbon steel began to corrode immediately. The anodic reaction involved the dissolution of Fe atoms: Fe – 2e– = Fe2+. Simultaneously, the cathodic reaction resulted in the hydrogen evolution: 2 H+ + 2e– = H2↑. These anodic and cathodic reactions led to the continuous corrosion of the metal surface. In addition, these processes were further accelerated by Cl ions in the solution.

Following the incorporation of GQDs (Fig. 15 b), the corrosion of steel was effectively inhibited. In detail, GQDs with substantial O-containing groups (determined by FTIR and XPS) possessed excellent dispersion stability in 1 M HCl. Well-dispersed GQDs-based inhibitors parallelly adsorbed on the carbon steel surface, enabling their coverage area, evidenced by MD simulation. And the parallel adsorption facilitated the formation of coordination bonds, via electron transfer between the lone pair electrons of GQDs and the unoccupied 3d orbital of Fe atoms (determined from DFT calculations). This led to the fact that GQDs-based inhibitors adsorbed onto the steel surface through a combination of physical and chemical interactions (tested by isotherm analysis). The adsorbed GQDs resulted in a more protective iron oxide layer to resist HCl corrosion (confirmed by XPS). Consequently, the anodic metal dissolution and the cathodic hydrogen evolution were significantly suppressed, thus improving inhibition performance, as testified by the weight loss test, EIS, and PDP measurements. Eventually, fewer corrosion products were formed on the steel surface (confirmed by SEM, EDS, and 3D morphology).

In contrast to GQDs, the addition of NGQDs induced an improving inhibiting effect (Fig. 15 c). This was ascribed to the fact that N-doping endowed NGQDs with better charge-donating ability and stronger adsorption on the steel surface. The more effectively adsorbed NGQDs contributed to forming a better protective iron oxide layer. Under this premise, the anodic and cathodic reactions of carbon steel were more effectively inhibited. Consequently, the corrosion products were distinctly reduced, and the steel surface became smoother and more homogeneous.

In a word, the inhibition mechanism of GQDs and NGQDs-based corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in 1 M HCl was attributed to their physical and chemical adsorption on the carbon steel surface, forming a more protective layer of iron oxides, thus effectively suppressing anode and cathode reactions. Besides, N doping further enhanced the charge-donating and adsorption capacities of NGQDs on the steel surface, bringing about the generation of a better protective iron oxides layer.

In this work, zero-dimensional carbon-based GQDs and NGQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method without any post-treatment, and innovatively served as novel green, efficient corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel. More importantly, their inhibition mechanism, accompanied by the inhibiting enhancement mechanism induced by N incorporation, was comprehensively uncovered. The main conclusions were as follows.
1.
GQDs and NGQDs, with average diameters of less than 3 nm, high crystalline carbon cores, and multitudes of functional groups, were successfully prepared. In stark contrast to GO, GQDs and NGQDs without necessitating any supplementary treatment or surface modification possessed smaller sizes, improved monodispersity, and higher water solubility. This was attributed to their abundant O-/N-containing groups. More importantly, GQDs and NGQDs were equipped with excellent long-term dispersion stability in 1 M HCl solutions, laying a solid foundation for their potential application as novel, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors.
2.
The corrosion inhibition performance through weight loss test, EIS, and PDP measurements evidenced that GQDs and NGQDs effectively inhibited the carbon steel corrosion in 1 M HCl. The inhibition efficiency of GQDs/NGQDs at 200 mg/L concentration reached 83.32%/89.25% confirmed by weight loss test, which was comparable to the modified GO inhibitors. In addition, NGQDs held higher inhibition behaviors than GQDs, revealing that N doping was an efficient way to improve the corrosion inhibition performance of GQDs.
3.
The inhibition mechanism of GQDs and NGQDs was investigated through corrosion morphology, adsorption isotherm analysis, and theoretical calculation. That was GQDs and NGQDs-based inhibitors adsorbed onto the steel surface through a combination of physical and chemical adsorption, forming a more protective layer of iron oxides, thus effectively suppressing anode and cathode reactions. Moreover, N doping significantly enhanced the charge-donating ability and adsorption capacity of NGQDs with the steel surface, resulting in a superior inhibition performance.

This work introduced a kind of promising environmentally friendly, cost-efficient corrosion inhibitor. These findings above would contribute to the advancement of corrosion inhibition theory, thus paving the way for future research in the development of novel corrosion inhibitors.
